feedback.text: For decades, any type of software was offered either as a physical media or a digital service. Most of these software offers, including video games and live service games, had written policies and rules as to what they are and how you can use them after you paid for them. Unfortunately, in recent years, certain software and video game developers and/or publisher studios, decided that their product will be a digital subscribtion service, disguised as a product which can be purchased as a copy, that can be taken away from you under many reasons. This resulted in consumers losing their software copies or losing access to the software. These business practices have been ongoing for over a decade, and there are many examples. I used to love playing a video game, called: Need For Speed World. The game was released on July 27, 2010. On April 15, 2015, the developer EA Games, has announced that the game servers will permanently shutdown on July 14, 2015. While players were aware that the game was online-only from the beginning, it was only after the shutdown was announced, that the majority of players realised that this is a new problem. The game is not just shutting down it's online services, but by doing so, it will be inaccessible to anyone. Because it was designed to be online all the time. So as a live service game, once servers are offline, the game cannot be played. While the game was marketed as Free To Play, it had standard, fictional in-game currency (which was earned by playing the game, winning races, etc.) and horrible "premium" in-game currency called "Speedboost", which could be bought with real currency. More detailed information and sources are available on "stopkillinggames" wiki. While the gaming community had luck and found certain members that managed to reverse-engineer the game and is now again available completely free, without real currency purchases. There are hundres, maybe even thousands of currently active games that will be shutdown the same way, as so many have before. We, as a community, have put effort into spreading the word over the globe about these issues. And we have managed to sign over 1,000,000 signatures for the European Citizens' Initiative, titled: Stop Destroying Videogames I and many other citizens of EU, would ask you to consider preventing another such bad consumer practice within the Digital Fairness Act: "Publishers permanently disabling video games they have already sold". Thank you for reading my feedback.
